Fried rice with beef and egg

english: Fried rice with beef and egg
malay: Nasi Goreng Daging Lembu dan Telur

food_subgroup: Fried rice dishes with meat and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific beef-and-egg fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried rice as a category; no specific beef-and-egg variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for beef and egg fried rice. No suitable parent concept identified for this prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of beef and egg fried rice as served in a Singaporean hawker stall or restaurant, approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, with Meat and Egg — HPB Singapore Foodo / FCID composite reference; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried rice with meat (NDB 22904) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO-INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo does not publish a specific 'beef and egg fried rice' entry. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo fried rice with meat and egg composite data, cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy NDB 22904 (fried rice, meat) and FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice entries. Beef substitution for generic meat is nutritionally minor at the macro level. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a standard Singaporean-style preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — fried rice analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef and egg fried rice. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base, stir-fried with mixed ingredients), which reports GI values in the range 48–64 depending on preparation. A central estimate of 55 is used, consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care) international GI tables for Asian fried rice dishes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 84.0) / 100 = 46.2, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 24.0 × 3.5 = 84.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1330 mg per standard 350 g serving) from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g and request reduced soy sauce, as a full serving provides approximately 1330 mg sodium — more than half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~46 GL units) driven by a large portion of white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) to limit glycemic load, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the white rice base contributes a substantial carbohydrate load of approximately 84 g per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.6 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from soy sauce seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish should be consumed occasionally and in reduced portions, as the combination of saturated fat from beef and egg and high sodium from soy sauce may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef and Egg Fried Rice (鸡蛋牛肉炒饭, Jī dàn niúròu chǎofàn) is a popular Singaporean Chinese stir-fried rice dish prepared in a wok over high heat. The dish combines day-old cooked white rice with sliced or minced beef, scrambled or beaten egg, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and spring onion. The high-heat wok technique produces the characteristic 'wok hei' (breath of the wok) aroma and slightly smoky flavour. The texture is fluffy and slightly chewy, with tender beef pieces and soft egg distributed throughout the rice. It is a common hawker centre and zi char dish in Singapore, served as a one-dish meal. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 613 kcal, 25 g protein, 20 g fat, and 84 g carbohydrate, with a high sodium content of approximately 1330 mg from soy sauce. The glycemic load per serving is high (~46 GL units), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains egg, soy, and gluten allergens. It is generally not certified halal due to the use of beef from non-halal sources in many establishments, though halal-certified versions exist.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for beef and egg fried rice exists. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo fried rice with meat and egg composite data, cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA references. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fried rice. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact match for this specific dish.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy composite sources used for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary composite sources. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary composite sources. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g per 100 g based on minor contributions from beef fat; this is a plausible low estimate consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked beef. The is_halal flag is set to false as the dish as served at Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles cannot be confirmed halal-certified; halal-certified variants exist at other establishments. GI category is 'low' per the Veritas threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low), noting that the glycemic load per serving is nonetheless high due to the large carbohydrate portion in a standard 350 g plate.

improvement_actions_taken: Assigned canonical English food name 'Beef and Egg Fried Rice' replacing menu-style input name.
Added Chinese simplified (鸡蛋牛肉炒饭), Chinese traditional (雞蛋牛肉炒飯), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.
Sourced nutrient values from HPB Foodo / USDA SR Legacy / FAO-INFOODS SEA composite with full adaptation notes.
Set GI = 55 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for fried rice; gi_category = 'low' per ≤55 threshold rule.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving = 46.2 based on 350 g serving and 24.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g.
Used FoodOn FOODON_00002694 and FoodEx2 A0FJP as closest_parent ontology anchors with gap notes; SNOMED CT set to none with gap note.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes field.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all language-specific strings appear verbatim in flat aliases.
Added Type 2 Diabetes health context with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' and machine key 'type2_diabetes'.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with preparation, taste, texture, cultural context, and clinical relevance.
